mappings.rationaleAI: Contemporary India covering the aftermath of British imperial power including India's emergence as a global power (C). Addresses democratic governance, caste dynamics, and positive discrimination as legacies of British colonial policies (C.3, C.4).

Gandhi's development of nonviolent resistance and leadership of Indian independence from British rule, directly covering the emergence of Mahatma Gandhi and the adoption of his noncooperation policy during and after World War I (C.4).

Rise and governance of the British Empire covering the East India Company's role in establishing British settlements and trading posts (A.3.a), Anglo-French rivalry (A.3.b), Clive's establishment of British control in Bengal (A.3.c). Extension of British power through company governance (B.1), securing supremacy in Bengal (B.1.a), Warren Hastings's administration (B.1.b), expansion and consolidation of control (B.1.d), administrative policy organization (B.1.e), and political effects of British influence (B.2). British imperial power including social and economic policies (C.1).

Empire as a political formation covering British colonial governance, theories of colonialism and decolonization, and the aftermath of imperialism -- broadly relevant to European activity in India (A), extension of British power (B), and British imperial power (C).

Hinduism covering the East India Company and Indian National Congress in shaping modern Hindu identity -- the political, social, and cultural effects of British influence (B.2). Hindu nationalism as part of the beginning of Indian nationalism (C.3).
Islam in the Indian subcontinent relevant to the founding of the nationalist Muslim League, Hindu-Muslim relations, and the role of Muslim identity in the independence movement (C.3).
Sikhism as a religion founded in 15th-century Punjab, with history of Sikh martyrdom and militarization relevant to the completion of British annexation of the Sikh kingdom in the 1840s (B.1.f).

Buddhism's establishment in Ceylon through conversion in the 3rd century BC, providing context for understanding Ceylon under foreign rulers (D.1) and Tibet's Buddhist administration under the Manchus (E.1).
Tibetan Buddhist development covering the institution of the Dalai Lama and Chinese suppression, relevant to the decline of Chinese influence in Tibet and administration under the Manchus (E.1).

Postcolonialism challenging Western-centric perspectives, covering experiences of colonized societies in Asia -- the political, social, and cultural effects of British influence (B.2) and the broader dynamics of British imperial power (C).

Nationalism as a political ideology relevant to the beginning of Indian nationalism in the late 19th century, formation of the Indian National Congress, and the emergence of national self-determination movements (C.3).

Transition from colonial empires to independent nation-states, covering anti-colonial activism including Nehru's leadership, relevant to Indian nationalism (C.3) and the aftermath of World War I leading toward independence (C.4).

World War I (1914-1918) directly relevant to India's contribution to the war effort, anti-British activity in its aftermath, and the political consequences leading to the Government of India Act (C.4).
Indian philosophical traditions providing the intellectual framework within which the cultural effects of British influence were debated and contested (B.2).

Pakistan as a postcolonial nation created from British India, covering the interplay of Islam, regional identity, and colonial legacy -- the founding of the nationalist Muslim League and Hindu-Muslim relations (C.3), and partition-era tensions emerging from the World War I era (C.4).

Eighteenth-century Britain covering the East India Company, imperial expansion, and mercantile policy -- establishment of British settlements and trading posts (A.3.a), Anglo-French rivalry (A.3.b), and the growth of company political power (B.1).
Diplomatic history relevant to British foreign policy in India, including conflicts over the northwest frontier and international relations during the imperial period (C.2).
